CHAPTER I: FEGLI FUNDAMENTALS

Federal Life Insurance Reality Check

FEGLI premiums skyrocket in retirement while your coverage needs typically decrease. Most federal employees are over-insured and don't realize private coverage could save them thousands annually.

Analyze My FEGLI

"Most federal employees are paying for life insurance they no longer need."

— Mike Stevens, IAR

On This Page

  • Coverage Options
  • Retirement Costs
  • Option B Problem
  • Private Alternatives
  • Coverage Analysis
  • Optimization Strategy
CHAPTER II: COVERAGE OPTIONS

Understanding Your FEGLI Options

FEGLI offers four different types of coverage, each with different costs, benefits, and implications for retirement. Understanding each option is essential for making informed decisions about your life insurance strategy.

Basic FEGLI

Annual Salary + $2,000

Automatic coverage for all federal employees. Reduces by 75% in retirement but premiums end. The foundation of federal life insurance.

Option A

$10,000 Fixed Coverage

Standard additional coverage. Relatively affordable throughout career and retirement. Premiums increase with age but coverage stays level.

Option B

1-5x Annual Salary

The expensive one. Affordable during career but premiums can exceed $500-800/month in retirement. This is where most savings opportunities exist.

Option C

Family Coverage

Coverage for spouse and children. Affordable for young families but becomes expensive as you age. Consider term life on family members instead.

$3,200 Average Annual FEGLI Savings
75% Basic FEGLI Reduction in Retirement
$800 Max Monthly Option B Premium
CHAPTER III: RETIREMENT IMPACT

The Retirement Cost Explosion

FEGLI premiums increase dramatically in retirement because they're based on your age, not your federal employment status. While your income drops in retirement, your insurance costs can skyrocket — creating a dangerous squeeze on your budget.

Option B Premium Progression (5x Salary Coverage)

Age Monthly Premium Annual Cost Coverage Amount Cost per $1,000
Age 45 $45 $540 $500,000 $1.08
Age 55 $112 $1,344 $500,000 $2.69
Age 65 $280 $3,360 $500,000 $6.72
Age 70 $520 $6,240 $500,000 $12.48
Age 75 $720 $8,640 $500,000 $17.28

The Retirement Squeeze

By age 70, a federal retiree with 5x Option B coverage could pay over $6,000 annually for life insurance — when their income has dropped significantly and their coverage needs have likely decreased. This is when most retirees have paid off their mortgage and their children are financially independent.

The irony: when you most needed life insurance (young family, mortgage, limited savings), FEGLI was affordable. When you least need it (retirement, paid-off house, grown children), it becomes prohibitively expensive.

CHAPTER IV: THE OPTION B TRAP

Why Option B Becomes a Problem

Option B (1-5 times salary) is where most federal employees get trapped. It's reasonably priced during your career but becomes outrageously expensive in retirement — precisely when your coverage needs have typically diminished.

The Option B Lifecycle

Early Career

✓

Young family, mortgage, affordable premiums. Option B makes sense.

Mid-Career

?

Premiums rising, but coverage still needed. Time to evaluate alternatives.

Retirement

✗

Kids grown, house paid off, high premiums. Usually time to reduce or drop.

The trap occurs because many federal employees elect high Option B coverage early in their career and never revisit the decision. They get comfortable with automatic payroll deductions and don't realize how expensive the coverage becomes in retirement.

From the book examples: Federal employees who kept Option B into retirement often spent $50,000-$100,000 more on life insurance over their retirement years than necessary, money that could have enhanced their retirement lifestyle or been left to their heirs.

CHAPTER V: ALTERNATIVES

Private Insurance Comparison

Private life insurance offers several advantages over FEGLI, especially for healthy federal employees. The key is getting coverage while you're still young and healthy, before health issues make you uninsurable.

Feature FEGLI Private Term Life Winner
Medical Underwriting None required Required FEGLI
Premiums (Healthy 40-year-old) $45/month $25/month Private
Premiums (70-year-old) $520/month Policy expires Private
Coverage Flexibility Limited options Customizable Private
Portability Tied to federal employment Fully portable Private
Conversion Options Limited Good Private

The optimal strategy for many federal employees: Keep Basic FEGLI (it's free in retirement), replace expensive Option B with level-premium term life insurance that expires when coverage is no longer needed (typically age 65-70).

50% Potential Savings with Private Insurance
20 Years Typical Term Length
$500k Common Coverage Amount
CHAPTER VI: NEEDS ANALYSIS

How Much Coverage Do You Actually Need?

Most federal employees elect life insurance based on what's available rather than what they actually need. A proper needs analysis considers your family situation, debts, income replacement needs, and existing assets.

Coverage Needs Calculation

Financial Obligations

  • + Mortgage balance
  • + Other debts
  • + Children's education costs
  • + Income replacement (5-10 years)
  • + Final expenses

Existing Assets

  • - Emergency savings
  • - TSP account
  • - Other investments
  • - Spouse's income/benefits
  • - FERS survivor annuity

Coverage Needed = Obligations - Assets

As you approach retirement, this equation typically shifts dramatically. Your mortgage gets paid down, your children become financially independent, your TSP grows, and your spouse may qualify for your FERS survivor annuity — all reducing your life insurance needs.

CHAPTER VII: OPTIMIZATION

FEGLI Optimization Strategy

The optimal FEGLI strategy evolves throughout your career. What makes sense at age 30 with young children may be completely inappropriate at age 60 approaching retirement. Regular review and adjustment are essential.

Recommended Strategy by Life Stage

Early Career (20s-30s)

  • Keep Basic FEGLI
  • Consider moderate Option B
  • Compare with private term life
  • Option C for family coverage

Mid-Career (40s-50s)

  • Review Option B costs vs. needs
  • Consider private insurance while healthy
  • Plan reduction strategy for retirement
  • Evaluate spousal coverage needs

Pre-Retirement (55+)

  • Significantly reduce Option B
  • Keep Basic FEGLI (free in retirement)
  • Consider final expenses coverage only
  • Plan for survivor benefit coordination

The key insight: FEGLI is designed for convenience, not optimization. Most federal employees benefit from a hybrid approach — using FEGLI strategically while supplementing with private coverage when appropriate.

Our analysis typically finds federal employees can save $2,000-$5,000 annually by optimizing their life insurance strategy, while often maintaining better coverage for their actual needs.

CHAPTER VIII: COMMON QUESTIONS

FEGLI Planning FAQ

Everything you need to know about federal life insurance and private alternatives.

Analyze My Coverage

Basic FEGLI reduces by 75% in retirement but remains free (no premium). Optional coverages are where costs can become problematic. Option A continues at $10,000 of coverage, and premiums increase with age. Option B (1-5 times salary) continues at full value, but premiums increase dramatically — often several hundred dollars per month for older retirees. Option C (family coverage) also continues with age-based premium increases. The key issue: while coverage continues, you're paying much higher rates in retirement when you may no longer need the same coverage level you had during your working years.

It depends on your health, age, and coverage needs. FEGLI's advantages: no medical underwriting, guaranteed issue, and convenience. Private insurance advantages: often lower premiums (especially for healthy individuals), fixed-term coverage that ends when you no longer need it, and potentially larger death benefits. Many federal employees benefit from keeping Basic FEGLI (it's free in retirement anyway) while replacing expensive Option B coverage with a lower-cost private term policy. We recommend getting a private insurance quote to compare — if you're healthy, you may be paying far more than necessary.

Option B is additional life insurance coverage equal to 1, 2, 3, 4, or 5 times your annual salary. During your working years, it's relatively affordable. The problem arises in retirement: Option B continues at full value (based on your salary at retirement), but premiums are based on your age and increase every 5 years. For a 70-year-old retiree with high Option B coverage, premiums can exceed $500-800 per month. By that age, many retirees no longer have the same coverage needs (mortgage paid off, kids financially independent), making these premiums difficult to justify.

Yes. You're not required to keep FEGLI, and you can cancel optional coverages at any time (though you may not be able to re-enroll later without a life event). Many federal employees choose to keep Basic FEGLI since it's free in retirement, but replace costly Option B with a private term life insurance policy that ends when coverage is no longer needed. Private insurance requires medical underwriting, so your health, age, and lifestyle affect eligibility and pricing. We can help you compare options and determine if private coverage makes sense for your situation.

You should review FEGLI at major life events: marriage, divorce, birth of children, home purchase, mortgage payoff, and as you approach retirement (ideally 5-10 years before). Many federal employees elect Option B early in their career when they have young children and a mortgage, then never revisit the decision. By retirement, circumstances have changed dramatically — kids are grown, the house is paid off, and the need for large life insurance coverage has diminished. Reviewing coverage every few years ensures you're not over-insured and overpaying for protection you no longer need.

position: relative; border-bottom: 3px solid transparent; } .related-post-card:hover { border-bottom-color: var(--secondary); transform: translateY(-2px); } .related-post-date { font-family: var(--font-mono); font-size: 0.75rem; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.05em; color: var(--on-surface-variant); margin-bottom: 0.75rem; } .related-post-title { font-family: var(--font-body); font-size: 1.25rem; margin: 0 auto; padding: 0 2rem; } .service-cta h2 { font-family: var(--font-serif-display); font-style: italic; font-size: 2.5rem; font-weight: 400; margin-bottom: 1rem; } .service-cta p { font-family: var(--font-body); font-size: 1.125rem; line-height: 1.6; margin-bottom: 2rem; opacity: 0.9; } .service-cta-button { display: inline-flex; align-items: center; gap: 0.5rem; font-family: var(--font-body); font-weight: 600; font-size: 1rem; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.05em; padding: 1rem 2rem; background: white; color: var(--primary); border-radius: 16px; text-decoration: none; transition: all 0.2s ease; } .service-cta-button:hover { background: var(--surface-container-low); transform: translateY(-2px); box-shadow: 0 8px 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.15); } /* Responsive Design */ @media (max-width: 768px) { .service-header { padding: 2rem 0 3rem; } .service-title { font-size: 2.5rem; } .service-subtitle { font-size: 1.125rem; } .related-posts-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } .related-posts { padding: 3rem 0 4rem; } .service-cta { padding: 3rem 0; } .service-cta h2 { font-size: 2rem; } }

FEGLI ANALYSIS

Federal Life Insurance Planning

FEGLI premiums skyrocket in retirement. Most federal employees can get better coverage at lower cost through private insurance. We analyze your needs and compare options.

CHAPTER I: FEGLI FUNDAMENTALS

Federal Life Insurance Reality Check

FEGLI premiums skyrocket in retirement while your coverage needs typically decrease. Most federal employees are over-insured and don't realize private coverage could save them thousands annually.

Analyze My FEGLI

"Most federal employees are paying for life insurance they no longer need."

— Mike Stevens, IAR

On This Page

  • Coverage Options
  • Retirement Costs
  • Option B Problem
  • Private Alternatives
  • Coverage Analysis
  • Optimization Strategy
CHAPTER II: COVERAGE OPTIONS

Understanding Your FEGLI Options

FEGLI offers four different types of coverage, each with different costs, benefits, and implications for retirement. Understanding each option is essential for making informed decisions about your life insurance strategy.

Basic FEGLI

Annual Salary + $2,000

Automatic coverage for all federal employees. Reduces by 75% in retirement but premiums end. The foundation of federal life insurance.

Option A

$10,000 Fixed Coverage

Standard additional coverage. Relatively affordable throughout career and retirement. Premiums increase with age but coverage stays level.

Option B

1-5x Annual Salary

The expensive one. Affordable during career but premiums can exceed $500-800/month in retirement. This is where most savings opportunities exist.

Option C

Family Coverage

Coverage for spouse and children. Affordable for young families but becomes expensive as you age. Consider term life on family members instead.

$3,200 Average Annual FEGLI Savings
75% Basic FEGLI Reduction in Retirement
$800 Max Monthly Option B Premium
CHAPTER III: RETIREMENT IMPACT

The Retirement Cost Explosion

FEGLI premiums increase dramatically in retirement because they're based on your age, not your federal employment status. While your income drops in retirement, your insurance costs can skyrocket — creating a dangerous squeeze on your budget.

Option B Premium Progression (5x Salary Coverage)

Age Monthly Premium Annual Cost Coverage Amount Cost per $1,000
Age 45 $45 $540 $500,000 $1.08
Age 55 $112 $1,344 $500,000 $2.69
Age 65 $280 $3,360 $500,000 $6.72
Age 70 $520 $6,240 $500,000 $12.48
Age 75 $720 $8,640 $500,000 $17.28

The Retirement Squeeze

By age 70, a federal retiree with 5x Option B coverage could pay over $6,000 annually for life insurance — when their income has dropped significantly and their coverage needs have likely decreased. This is when most retirees have paid off their mortgage and their children are financially independent.

The irony: when you most needed life insurance (young family, mortgage, limited savings), FEGLI was affordable. When you least need it (retirement, paid-off house, grown children), it becomes prohibitively expensive.

CHAPTER IV: THE OPTION B TRAP

Why Option B Becomes a Problem

Option B (1-5 times salary) is where most federal employees get trapped. It's reasonably priced during your career but becomes outrageously expensive in retirement — precisely when your coverage needs have typically diminished.

The Option B Lifecycle

Early Career

✓

Young family, mortgage, affordable premiums. Option B makes sense.

Mid-Career

?

Premiums rising, but coverage still needed. Time to evaluate alternatives.

Retirement

✗

Kids grown, house paid off, high premiums. Usually time to reduce or drop.

The trap occurs because many federal employees elect high Option B coverage early in their career and never revisit the decision. They get comfortable with automatic payroll deductions and don't realize how expensive the coverage becomes in retirement.

From the book examples: Federal employees who kept Option B into retirement often spent $50,000-$100,000 more on life insurance over their retirement years than necessary, money that could have enhanced their retirement lifestyle or been left to their heirs.

CHAPTER V: ALTERNATIVES

Private Insurance Comparison

Private life insurance offers several advantages over FEGLI, especially for healthy federal employees. The key is getting coverage while you're still young and healthy, before health issues make you uninsurable.

Feature FEGLI Private Term Life Winner
Medical Underwriting None required Required FEGLI
Premiums (Healthy 40-year-old) $45/month $25/month Private
Premiums (70-year-old) $520/month Policy expires Private
Coverage Flexibility Limited options Customizable Private
Portability Tied to federal employment Fully portable Private
Conversion Options Limited Good Private

The optimal strategy for many federal employees: Keep Basic FEGLI (it's free in retirement), replace expensive Option B with level-premium term life insurance that expires when coverage is no longer needed (typically age 65-70).

50% Potential Savings with Private Insurance
20 Years Typical Term Length
$500k Common Coverage Amount
CHAPTER VI: NEEDS ANALYSIS

How Much Coverage Do You Actually Need?

Most federal employees elect life insurance based on what's available rather than what they actually need. A proper needs analysis considers your family situation, debts, income replacement needs, and existing assets.

Coverage Needs Calculation

Financial Obligations

  • + Mortgage balance
  • + Other debts
  • + Children's education costs
  • + Income replacement (5-10 years)
  • + Final expenses

Existing Assets

  • - Emergency savings
  • - TSP account
  • - Other investments
  • - Spouse's income/benefits
  • - FERS survivor annuity

Coverage Needed = Obligations - Assets

As you approach retirement, this equation typically shifts dramatically. Your mortgage gets paid down, your children become financially independent, your TSP grows, and your spouse may qualify for your FERS survivor annuity — all reducing your life insurance needs.

CHAPTER VII: OPTIMIZATION

FEGLI Optimization Strategy

The optimal FEGLI strategy evolves throughout your career. What makes sense at age 30 with young children may be completely inappropriate at age 60 approaching retirement. Regular review and adjustment are essential.

Recommended Strategy by Life Stage

Early Career (20s-30s)

  • Keep Basic FEGLI
  • Consider moderate Option B
  • Compare with private term life
  • Option C for family coverage

Mid-Career (40s-50s)

  • Review Option B costs vs. needs
  • Consider private insurance while healthy
  • Plan reduction strategy for retirement
  • Evaluate spousal coverage needs

Pre-Retirement (55+)

  • Significantly reduce Option B
  • Keep Basic FEGLI (free in retirement)
  • Consider final expenses coverage only
  • Plan for survivor benefit coordination

The key insight: FEGLI is designed for convenience, not optimization. Most federal employees benefit from a hybrid approach — using FEGLI strategically while supplementing with private coverage when appropriate.

Our analysis typically finds federal employees can save $2,000-$5,000 annually by optimizing their life insurance strategy, while often maintaining better coverage for their actual needs.

CHAPTER VIII: COMMON QUESTIONS

FEGLI Planning FAQ

Everything you need to know about federal life insurance and private alternatives.

Analyze My Coverage

Basic FEGLI reduces by 75% in retirement but remains free (no premium). Optional coverages are where costs can become problematic. Option A continues at $10,000 of coverage, and premiums increase with age. Option B (1-5 times salary) continues at full value, but premiums increase dramatically — often several hundred dollars per month for older retirees. Option C (family coverage) also continues with age-based premium increases. The key issue: while coverage continues, you're paying much higher rates in retirement when you may no longer need the same coverage level you had during your working years.

It depends on your health, age, and coverage needs. FEGLI's advantages: no medical underwriting, guaranteed issue, and convenience. Private insurance advantages: often lower premiums (especially for healthy individuals), fixed-term coverage that ends when you no longer need it, and potentially larger death benefits. Many federal employees benefit from keeping Basic FEGLI (it's free in retirement anyway) while replacing expensive Option B coverage with a lower-cost private term policy. We recommend getting a private insurance quote to compare — if you're healthy, you may be paying far more than necessary.

Option B is additional life insurance coverage equal to 1, 2, 3, 4, or 5 times your annual salary. During your working years, it's relatively affordable. The problem arises in retirement: Option B continues at full value (based on your salary at retirement), but premiums are based on your age and increase every 5 years. For a 70-year-old retiree with high Option B coverage, premiums can exceed $500-800 per month. By that age, many retirees no longer have the same coverage needs (mortgage paid off, kids financially independent), making these premiums difficult to justify.

Yes. You're not required to keep FEGLI, and you can cancel optional coverages at any time (though you may not be able to re-enroll later without a life event). Many federal employees choose to keep Basic FEGLI since it's free in retirement, but replace costly Option B with a private term life insurance policy that ends when coverage is no longer needed. Private insurance requires medical underwriting, so your health, age, and lifestyle affect eligibility and pricing. We can help you compare options and determine if private coverage makes sense for your situation.

You should review FEGLI at major life events: marriage, divorce, birth of children, home purchase, mortgage payoff, and as you approach retirement (ideally 5-10 years before). Many federal employees elect Option B early in their career when they have young children and a mortgage, then never revisit the decision. By retirement, circumstances have changed dramatically — kids are grown, the house is paid off, and the need for large life insurance coverage has diminished. Reviewing coverage every few years ensures you're not over-insured and overpaying for protection you no longer need.
